title: 第 15 節
en: It was formerly supposed that the psychological problem involved in fetishism could be explained by a mere reference to children playing with their dolls, or hitting the chair against which they had hit themselves. This explanation, however, has long been surrendered, for, even supposing that fetishism consisted only in ascribing to material objects life, activity, or personality, call it figurism, animism, personification, anthropomorphism, or anthropo- 124 LECTURE II.

pathism, the mere fact that children do the same as grown-up savages cannot possibly help us to solve the psychological problem. The fact, suppose it is a fact, would be as mysterious with children as with savages. Besides, though there is some truth in calling savages children, or children savages, we must here, too, learn to distinguish. Savages are children in some respects, but not in all. There is no savage who, on growing up, does not learn to distinguish between animate and inanimate objects, between a rope, for instance, and a serpent. To say that they remain childish on such a point is only to cheat ourselves with our own metaphors. On the other side, children, such as they now are, can help us but rarely to gain an idea of what primitive savages may have been. Our children, from the first awakening of their mental life, are surrounded by an atmosphere saturated with the thoughts of an advanced civilisation. A child, not taken in by a well-dressed doll, or so perfectly able to control himself as not to kick against a chair against which he had hit his head, would be a little philosopher rather than a savage, not yet emerging from fetishism. The circumstances or the surroundings are so totally different in the case of the savage and the child, that comparisons between the two must be carried out with the greatest care before they can claim any real scientific value.

I agree so far with the believers in primitive fetishism that if we are to explain religion as a uni versal property of mankind, we must explain it out of conditions which are universally present. Nor do I blame them if they decline to discuss the problem of the origin of religion with those who assume a IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION 1 125 primitive revelation, or a religious faculty which distinguishes man from the animal. Let us start, by all means, from common ground and from safe ground. Let us take man such as he is, possessing his five senses, and as yet without any knowledge except what is supplied to him by his five senses. No doubt that man can pick up a stone, or a bone, or a shell. But then we must ask the upholders of the primitive fetish theory, How do these people, when they have picked up their stone or their shell, pick up at the same time the concepts of a supernatural power, of spirit, of god, and of worship paid to some unseen being "?
The four steps.

We are told that there are four steps — the famous four steps — by which all this is achieved, and the origin of fetishism rendered perfectly intelligible. First, there is a sense of surprise ; secondly, an anthropopathic conception of the object which causes surprise ; thirdly, the admission of a causal connec tion between that object and certain effects, such as victory, rain, health ; fourthly, a recognition of the object as a power deserving of respect and worship. But is not this rather to hide the difficulties beneath a golden shower of words than to explain them ?

Granted that a man may be surprised at a stone or a shell, though they would seem to be the very last things to be surprised at ; but what is the meaning of taking an anthropopathic view of a stone or a shell 1 If we translate it into plain English it means neither more nor less than that, instead of taking a stone to be a stone like all other stones, we suppose that a particular stone is not an ordinary 126 LECTURE II.

stone, but endowed with the feelings of a man. Natural as this may sound, when clothed in technical language, when we use long names, such as anthropopathism, anthropomorphism, personification, figurism, nothing would really seem to do greater violence to common sense, or to our five senses, than to say that a stone is a stone, yet not quite a stone ; and again, that the stone is a man, yet not quite a man. I am fully aware that, after a long series of intermediate steps, such contradictions arise in the human mind, but they cannot spring up suddenly ; they are not there from the beginning, unless we admit disturbing influences much more extraordinary than a primeval revelation. It is the object of the science of religion to find out by what small and timid steps the human mind advanced from what is intelligible to what at first sight is almost beyond our comprehension. If we take for granted the verything that has to be explained ; if we once admit that it was perfectly natural for the primitive savage to look upon a stone as something human ; if we are satisfied with such words as anthropopathism, or animism, or figurism, — then all the rest no doubt is easy enough. The human stone has every right to be called superhuman, and that is not very far from divine ; nor need we wonder that the worship paid to such an object should be more than what is paid to either a stone or to a man — that it too should be superhuman, which is not very far from divine.
Fetishism not a primary form of religion.

My position then is simply this : It seems to me that those who believe in a primordial fetishism have IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION? 127 taken that for granted which has to be proved. They have taken for granted that every human being was miraculously endowed with the concept of what forms the predicate of every fetish, call it power, spirit, or god. They have taken for granted that casual objects, such as stones, shells, the tail of a lion, a tangle of hair, or any such rubbish, possess in themselves a theogonic or god-producing character, while the fact that all people, when they have once risen to the suspicion of something supersensuous, infinite, or divine, have perceived its presence after wards in merely casual and insignificant objects, has been entirely overlooked. They have taken for granted that there exists at present, or that there existed at any time, a religion entirely made up of fetishism ; or that, on the other hand, there is any religion which has kept itself entirely free from fetishism. My last and most serious objection, how ever, is that those who believe in fetishism as a primitive and universal form of religion, have often depended on evidence which no scholar, no historian, would feel justified to accept. We are justified therefore, I think, in surrendering the theory1 that fetishism either has or must have been the beginning of all religion, and we are bound to look elsewhere, if we wish to discover what were the sensuous impres sions that first filled the human mind with a suspicion of the supersensuous, the infinite, and the divine.

1 I am glad to find that both Dr. Happel, in his work ' Die Anlage des Menschen zur Religion,' 1878, and Professor Pfleiderer in his ' Religionsphilosophie,' just published, take nearly the same view of the Fetish-theory, THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA SO FAR AS IT SUPPLIES MATERIALS FOR THE STUDY OF THE ORIGIN OF RELIGION.
Usefulness of the study of literary religions.

INSTEAD of trying to study the origin of religion in the tertiary or quaternary strata of Africa, America and Australia, it seems far wiser to look first to countries where we find, not only the latest formations, the mere surface and detritus of religious growth, but where we can see and study some at least of the lower strata on which the superficial soil of religion reposes.

I know very weU that this study also has its difficulties, quite as much as the study of the re ligion of savage races, but the soil on which we have here to labour is deeper, and promises a richer harvest.

It is quite true that the historical documents of a religion never carry us very far. They fail us often just where they would be most instructive, near the first springs of the old stream. This is inevitable. No religion is of importance to the surrounding world in its first beginnings. It is THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 129 hardly noticed, so long as it is confined to the heart of one man and his twelve disciples. This applies to national religions still more than to what I call personal religions, the latter founded by known in dividuals, the former elaborated by the united efforts of a whole people. For many generations a national religion has no tangible form as a body of doctrine or ceremonies : it has hardly a name. We only know a religion, after it has assumed consistency and importance, and when it has become the in terest of certain individuals or of a whole class, to collect and to preserve for posterity whatever is known of its origin and first spreading. It is not by accident therefore, but by a law of human nature, that the accounts which we possess of the origin of religions, are almost always fabulous, never historical in the strict sense of the word.
Growth of religious ideas in Judaism, Zoroastrianism, etc.

But though we can nowhere watch the first vital movements of a nascent religion, we can in some countries observe the successive growth of religious ideas. Among the savages of Africa, Ame rica, and Australia this is impossible. It is difficult enough to know what their religion is at present ; what it was in its origin, what it was even a thousand years ago, is entirely beyond our reach.

Many of the so-called book-religions also offer the same, or at least similar, difficulties. There are traces of growth and decay in the religion of the Jews, but they have to be discovered by patient study. The object, however, of most of the writers on the 0. T. seems to be to hide these traces rather 130 LECTUBE III.

than to display them. They wish to place the religion of the Jews before us as ready-made from the beginning, as perfect in all its parts, because revealed by God, and, if liable to corruption, at all events incapable of improvement. But that the Jewish monotheism was preceded by a polytheism ' on the other side of the flood and in Egypt/ is now admitted by most scholars, nor would it be easy to find in the same sacred code two more opposite sentiments than the rules and regulations for burnt offerings in Leviticus, and the words of the Psalmist (51. 1 6), 'For thou delightest not in sacrifice, else would I give it thee ; thou delightest not in burnt offerings. The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit : a broken and contrite heart, 0 God, thou wilt not despise.'
There is growth here, as evident as can be, how ever difficult it may seem to some students of re ligion to reconcile the idea of growth with the character of a revealed religion.

What applies to the religion of Moses, applies to that of Zoroaster. It is placed before us as a complete system from the first, revealed by Ahuramazda, proclaimed by Zarathustra. Minute scholar ship only has been able to discover some older elements in the Gathas, but with that exception, we find in the Avesta too, but few acknowledged traces of real growth.

With regard again to the religion and mythology of Greece and Italy, it would be extremely difficult to distinguish their infancy, their youth, and their manhood. We know that certain ideas, which we find in later writers, do not occur in Homer ; but it does not follow at all, that therefore such ideas are THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 131 all of later growth, or possess a secondary character. One myth may have belonged to one tribe, one god may have had his chief worship in one locality, and our becoming acquainted with these through a later poet, does not in the least prove their later origin. Besides, there is this great disadvantage in the study of the religion of the Greeks and Romans, that we do not possess anything really deserving the name of a sacred book.
Growth of religion in India.

No country can be compared to India as offering opportunities for a real study of the genesis and growth of religion. I say intentionally for the growth, not for the history of religion : for history, in the ordinary sense of the word, is almost un known in Indian literature. But what we can watch and study in India better than anywhere else is, how religious thoughts and religious lan guage arise, how they gain force, how they spread, changing their forms as they pass from mouth to mouth, from mind to mind, yet always retaining some faint contiguity with the spring from which they rose at first.

I do not think therefore that I am exaggerating when I say that the sacred books of India offer for a study of religion in general, and particularly for the study of the origin and growth of religion, the same peculiar and unexpected advantages which the language of India, Sanskrit, has offered for the study of the origin and growth of human speech. It is for that reason that I have selected the ancient religion of India to supply the historical illustrations of my own theory of the origin and growth of K 2 132 LECTURE III.
religion. That theory was suggested to me during a lifelong study of the sacred books of India ; it rests therefore on facts, though I am responsible for their interpretation.
The right position of the Veda in the science of religion.

Far be it from me to say that the origin and growth of religion must everywhere have been exactly the same as in India. Let us here too take a warning from the science of language. It is no longer denied that for throwing light on some of the darkest problems that have to be solved by the student of language, nothing is so useful as a critical study of Sanskrit. I go further, even, and maintain that, in order to comprehend fully the ways and means adopted by other languages, nothing is more advantageous than to be able to contrast them with the proceedings of Sanskrit. But to look for Sanskrit, as Bopp has done, in Malay, Polynesian, and Caucasian dialects, or to imagine that the grammatical expedients adopted by the Aryan languages are the only possible expedients for realising the objects of human speech, would be a fatal mistake ; and we must guard, from the very first, against a similar danger in a scientific study of the religions of mankind. When we have learnt how the ancient inhabitants of India gained their religious ideas, how they elaborated them, changed them, corrupted them, we may be allowed to say that possibly other people also may have started from the same beginnings, and may have passed through the same vicissitudes. But we shall never go beyond, or repeat the mistake of those THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 133 who, because they found, or imagined they found fetish-worship among the least cultivated races of Africa, America, and Australia, concluded that every uncultivated race must have started from fetishism in its religious career.
What then are the documents in which we can study the origin and growth of religion among the early Aryan settlers of India 1 Discovery of Sanskrit literature.

The discovery of the ancient literature of India must sound to most people like a fairy-tale rather than like a chapter of history, nor do I wonder that there is, or that there has been at least for a long time, a certain incredulity, with regard to the genuineness of that literature. The number of separate works in Sanskrit, of which manuscripts are still in existence, is now estimated to amount to about 1 0,000 \ What would Plato and Aristotle have said, if they had been told that at their time there existed in India, in that India which Alex ander had just discovered, if not conquered, an ancient literature far richer than anything they possessed at that time in Greece 1 1 Rajendralal Mitra, ' Catalogue of Sanskrit MSS. in the Library of the Asiatic Library of Bengal,' 1877, Preface, p. I. The India Office Library is said to contain 4093 separate codices ; the Bod leian 854, the Berlin library about the same number. The library of the Maharaja of Tanjore is estimated at upwards of 18000, in eleven distinct alphabets; the library of the Sanskrit College at Benares at 2000 ; the library of the Asiatic Society of Bengal at Calcutta at 3700 ; that of the Sanskrit College at Calcutta at 134 LECTURE lit.
Buddhism the frontier between ancient and modern literature in India.
zh: 過去人們曾以為，物神崇拜（fetishism）所涉及的心理學問題，只需提及兒童玩洋娃娃、或敲打他們自己撞到的椅子這類行為，便能加以解釋。然而，這種解釋早已被揚棄，因為即便假設物神崇拜僅在於將生命、活動或人格賦予物質對象——無論稱之為形象主義（figurism）、萬物有靈論（animism）、擬人化（personification）、神人同形論（anthropomorphism）或人形論（anthropo- 124 第二講）。

弗里德里希‧馬克斯‧穆勒 ……萬物有靈論（pathism）中，單憑兒童與成年野人行為相同這一事實，並不能幫助我們解決心理學問題。即便此事屬實，其之於兒童與之於野人同樣神秘。此外，雖稱野人為兒童、或兒童為野人確有幾分道理，但在此處我們仍須學會分辨。野人在某些方面如同兒童，卻非全然如此。沒有任何一個野人在成長過程中不會學會區分有生命與無生命之物，例如區分繩索與蛇。若說他們在這一點上始終幼稚，不過是以自身隱喻自欺欺人。另一方面，當今的兒童極少能幫助我們理解原始野人可能的面貌。我們的兒童從心智生活初醒之際，便已身處飽含先進文明思想的氛圍之中。一個不會被衣著華麗的玩偶所矇騙、或能完美自制以至於不會因撞到頭而踢打椅子的兒童，與其說是尚未脫離物神崇拜的野人，不如說是小哲學家。野人與兒童所處的環境或周遭條件全然不同，因此兩者之間的比較必須極其審慎，方能具備真正的科學價值。

我大致同意原始拜物教信徒的觀點：若要將宗教解釋為人類的普遍屬性，我們就必須從普遍存在的條件來解釋它。我也不責怪他們拒絕與那些假定存在原始啟示、或某種區分人與動物的宗教官能的人，討論宗教起源的問題。讓我們務必從共同且穩固的基礎出發。讓我們就從人類的本然狀態開始：擁有五種感官，且除了五種感官所提供的知識之外，一無所知。無疑地，人可以撿起一塊石頭、一根骨頭或一個貝殼。但接著我們必須追問原始拜物教理論的擁護者：當這些人撿起他們的石頭或貝殼時，他們是如何同時撿起超自然力量、靈、神，以及向某種不可見存在獻祭崇拜這些概念的？

我們現在必須區分宗教史上的四個連續階段。第一個階段，我們可以稱之為物質宗教（material religion）或自然宗教（physical religion），因為它將無限者（the Infinite）感知為存在於自然現象之中——在廣袤的天空、在太陽、在月亮、在星辰、在黎明、在風暴、在雷電、在海洋、在河流、在山巒、在樹木、在動物之中。第二個階段，我們可以稱之為人類宗教（human religion）或精神宗教（spiritual religion），因為它將無限者感知為存在於人類自身之中——在祖先、在英雄、在君王、在祭司、在先知之中。第三個階段是神話宗教（mythological religion），它將無限者感知為存在於神話的創造物之中——存在於那些既是自然力量又是人類屬性的化身的神祇之中。第四個階段是哲學宗教（philosophical religion），它將無限者感知為存在於純粹的思想之中——存在於那些超越一切名稱與形式的抽象概念之中。 然而，我們必須記住，這些階段並非總是以清晰的順序出現。它們經常重疊、交錯，並以最令人困惑的方式相互融合。在我們稱之為吠陀（Veda）的古代讚美詩中，我們可以觀察到宗教思想從物質階段到神話階段的過渡。在奧義書（Upanishad）中，我們看到了從神話階段到哲學階段的過渡。而在《舊約》中，我們看到了從人類宗教到一神教（monotheism）的過渡，儘管其中也夾雜著多神教（polytheism）與單一神教（henotheism）的殘餘。
我們被告知，要達成這一切並讓物神崇拜的起源變得完全可理解，需要經歷四個步驟——著名的四個步驟。首先，是驚奇感；其次，對引起驚奇的對象產生擬人化的概念；第三，承認該對象與某些結果（如勝利、雨水、健康）之間存在因果關聯；第四，將該對象視為值得尊敬與崇拜的力量。然而，這與其說是解釋這些困難，不如說是以華麗的言辭之雨將其掩蓋，不是嗎？
誠然，一個人可能對一塊石頭或一枚貝殼感到驚奇，儘管它們看似最不該令人驚奇之物；但對一塊石頭或一枚貝殼採取擬人觀（anthropopathic view）究竟有何意義？若將其譯為平實的英文，這意思不多不少正是：我們不把一塊石頭當作與其他所有石頭無異的石頭，反而假定某塊特定的石頭並非尋常之物。

石頭，卻被賦予了人的情感。這聽起來或許很自然，但一旦披上技術性的語言外衣，當我們使用「神人同感論」（anthropopathism）、「神人同形論」（anthropomorphism）、「擬人化」（personification）、「象徵論」（figurism）這類冗長名詞時，恐怕沒有什麼比說「石頭是石頭，卻又不完全是石頭；再者，石頭是人，卻又不完全是人」更違背常理，或更違揹我們的五官感受了。我完全明白，在經歷一長串的中間步驟之後，人類心智中會產生這類矛盾；但它們不可能突然湧現；它們並非一開始就存在，除非我們承認存在著比「原始啟示」更為奇特的幹擾因素。宗教學的目標，正是要找出人類心智是經由何等細微而審慎的步伐，從可理解的事物，進展到乍看之下幾乎超出我們理解範圍的事物。如果我們把一切有待解釋的事物都視為理所當然；如果我們一旦承認，原始野人將石頭視為某種具有人性的東西是完全自然的；如果我們滿足於「神人同感論」、「萬物有靈論」（animism）或「象徵論」這類詞彙——那麼，其餘一切無疑都輕而易舉了。這塊「人性之石」完全有資格被稱為「超人的」，而這離「神聖的」並不遙遠；我們也無須訝異，獻給這類對象的崇拜，應當會超越獻給單純一塊石頭或一個人的崇拜——它同樣也應是「超人的」，而這離「神聖的」同樣不遠。

然而，我們必須謹慎，切勿將物神崇拜（Fetishism）視為宗教的一種原始或初級形式。孔德（Comte）及其學派所提出的這一觀點，如今已被宗教學（the science of religion）領域中更為深入的研究所推翻。物神崇拜，就其最嚴格的意義而言，即對人造物或自然物——如一塊木頭、一顆石頭、一片貝殼或一顆牙齒——賦予其本身並不具備的內在神聖力量或靈性（spirit）的崇拜，此種現象並非宗教的起點。相反地，它更像是宗教在其發展過程中，因對更崇高、更純粹的神性概念產生誤解或退化而出現的一種產物。我們在那些已知最古老的宗教文獻中，例如《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）的讚歌裡，找不到任何物神崇拜的痕跡；那裡的諸神，如特尤斯（Dyaus）、阿耆尼（Agni）、因陀羅（Indra）等，雖然與自然現象緊密相連，但他們並非被視為一塊石頭或一棵樹，而是被視為活生生的、有位格的存在，是掌管天、火、風暴等領域的力量。因此，將物神崇拜視為宗教的普遍原初形式，是一種誤導。它更可能是一種後起的、墮落的現象，是當人們對「無限者」（the Infinite）的原始直覺變得模糊，且對神聖者的敬畏感轉移到偶然的、具體的物體上時所產生的結果。
我很高興發現，哈佩爾博士在其 1878 年著作《人類的宗教稟賦》（Die Anlage des Menschen zur Religion）中，以及普夫萊德雷爾教授在其剛出版的《宗教哲學》（Religionsphilosophie）中，對於物神崇拜理論幾乎採取了相同的觀點。**印度古代文獻——就其為宗教起源研究提供材料而言**。

研究文學宗教的用處，在於它們為我們提供了最清晰、最可靠的證據，用以追溯宗教思想的發展歷程。當我們審視那些被記錄下來的經典文本——如吠陀（Veda）、奧義書（Upanishad）或聖經——我們得以直接觀察人類如何從對自然力量的樸素崇拜，逐步演進至對無限者（the Infinite）的哲學思辨。這些文獻不僅保存了古代儀式與神話的原始面貌，更揭示了語言如何塑造神聖概念，甚至導致所謂「語言的疾病」（a disease of language），即神話的誤解與扭曲。 相較於僅依賴考古遺跡或民族誌報告，文學宗教的優勢在於其內在的連貫性與自我解釋能力。例如，梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中的頌歌展現了從多神教（polytheism）到單一神教（henotheism）乃至輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的過渡，而這些階段在口傳傳統中往往難以辨識。透過比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的方法，我們能系統性地分析雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）等不同語系中的神祇名稱——如特尤斯（Dyaus）與提婆（Deva）——從而追溯其共同的印歐根源。 因此，文學宗教的研究不僅是宗教學（the science of religion）的基石，更為語言科學（the science of language）提供了關鍵的佐證。它幫助我們理解，為何同一神聖概念在不同文化中會衍生出截然不同的崇拜形式，以及人類心靈如何在神聖文本的框架內，不斷追尋超越自身的終極真理。
與其試圖在非洲、美洲與澳洲的第三紀或第四紀地層中研究宗教的起源，更明智的做法似乎是先將目光投向那些我們不僅能找到最新形成物——即宗教生長的單純表層與碎屑——而且至少能看見並研究部分承載宗教表層土壤的下層地層的國度。
我非常清楚，這項研究也有其困難，其程度絲毫不亞於對野蠻種族宗教的研究；但我們在此必須耕耘的土壤更為深厚，也預示著更豐碩的收成。

宗教的歷史文獻確實從未將我們帶回非常久遠的源頭。它們往往在最能啟發我們之處，也就是古老河流最初的源頭附近，令我們失望。這是無可避免的。任何宗教在其最初萌芽階段，對周遭世界而言都無足輕重。**印度的古代文獻**（THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA）幾乎不為人所注意，只要它仍侷限於一人及其十二門徒的心中。這點對於民族宗教而言，比對我稱之為個人宗教者更為適用；後者由已知的個人所創立，前者則由整個民族齊心協力所精心構築。在許多世代中，民族宗教並無具體的教義或儀式體系作為其可觸及的形式：它甚至幾乎沒有名稱。我們只有在一個宗教具備了連貫性與重要性之後，並且當某些個人或整個階層開始有興趣為後世收集並保存一切關於其起源與最初傳播的知識時，才得以認識它。因此，我們所擁有關於宗教起源的記述，幾乎總是荒誕不經，從未嚴格意義上符合歷史，這並非出於偶然，而是人性的一條法則。

猶太教與瑣羅亞斯德教（Zoroastrianism）等宗教體系中，宗教觀唸的發展呈現出獨特的軌跡。這些宗教雖各自源於不同的文化脈絡，卻在歷史進程中展現出對神聖秩序、善惡二元論及終極救贖等核心議題的深刻思索。其思想演變不僅反映在經典文獻的層層累積上，更體現於儀式實踐與倫理規範的持續調整之中。透過比較這些宗教的發展歷程，我們得以窺見人類宗教意識從多神崇拜（polytheism）逐步邁向一神信仰（monotheism）的複雜辯證過程，而此過程亦與語言科學（the science of language）所揭示的思維結構變遷相互呼應。
但即便我們無從觀察任何新興宗教最初的生機脈動，在某些國度，我們仍能見證宗教觀唸的接續成長。在非洲、美洲與澳洲的野蠻民族中，這項觀察是不可能的。要知曉他們當前的信仰已屬不易；至於其起源為何，甚至一千年前的樣貌為何，則完全超出我們所能觸及的範圍。
許多所謂的「經典宗教」也呈現出相同或至少相似的困難。猶太人的宗教中存有成長與衰敗的痕跡，但這些必須透過耐心的研究才能發現。然而，大多數舊約聖經學者的目標，似乎是要隱藏這些痕跡，而非揭示它們。

……而非展示它們。他們希望將猶太人的宗教呈現在我們面前，視其為從起初便已完備、各部分皆臻至善，因係上帝所啟示，縱使可能敗壞，也絕無改進之餘地。然而，如今多數學者都已承認，猶太人的一神教（monotheism）之前存在著「洪水彼岸與埃及境內」的多神教（polytheism）；在同一部聖典中，也很難找到比利未記中關於燔祭的條例與詩篇作者（51:16）的話語更為對立的兩種情懷——「因為你不喜愛祭物，否則我必獻上；你不喜愛燔祭。上帝所要的祭就是憂傷的靈；憂傷痛悔的心，上帝啊，你必不輕看。」
這裡的成長顯而易見，無論對某些宗教研究者而言，將成長的概念與啟示宗教的特性調和起來看似多麼困難。
適用於摩西宗教的原則，同樣適用於瑣羅亞斯德的宗教。它從一開始就以一套完整的體系呈現在我們面前，由阿胡拉‧馬茲達（Ahuramazda）啟示，經由查拉圖斯特拉（Zarathustra）宣揚。唯有細密的學術研究，才能在《伽薩》（Gathas）中發現一些更古老的元素；但除此之外，我們在《阿維斯塔》（Avesta）中也同樣發現，真正發展的痕跡極少獲得承認。

再就希臘與羅馬的宗教及神話而言，要區分其幼年、青年與成年階段，極其困難。我們知道，某些在後世作家作品中出現的觀念，並未見於荷馬；但這絕不意味著，這些觀念因此便是**印度古代文獻**。131 所有這些觀念皆屬後起，或帶有次要性質。某個神話可能歸屬於某個部族，某位神祇可能主要在某一地區受到崇拜，而我們透過後世詩人得知這些內容，絲毫不證明它們起源較晚。此外，研究希臘與羅馬宗教時，還有一項重大不利之處：我們並未擁有任何真正堪稱「聖書」的文獻。

在印度，宗教的發展呈現出一種獨特的連續性，幾乎可以說是宗教思想從其最原始的萌芽到最精緻的形而上學體系之自然演進的活標本。我們在此所見的，並非如閃族宗教史中那般因先知或改革者的出現而產生的劇烈斷裂，而是一種緩慢、有機的內部發展。吠陀時期最初的頌歌，充滿了對自然現象——如黎明（Ushas）、風暴（Maruts）、尤其是蒼穹與雷雨之神因陀羅（Indra）——的樸素驚嘆與祈求。這些詩人尚未明確區分神聖與世俗，他們以一種近乎孩童般的直接性，將周遭的力量人格化為提婆（Deva），即「光明者」。然而，即便在此早期階段，一種對超越個別神祇之「無限者」（the Infinite）的模糊追尋已然浮現。梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中那首著名的〈創造之歌〉（Nāsadīya Sūkta）便以懷疑的語調探問世界的起源，最終歸結於「唯一者」（Tad Ekam）——那個無氣息而自存、超越眾神的存在。這種思想傾向，在後期的奧義書（Upanishad）中得到了徹底的哲學化發展，將「梵」（Brahman）確立為宇宙的終極實在與個體靈魂（Ātman）的本質同一。 然而，這種看似直線的發展，實際上伴隨著一個重要的中間階段，即穆勒所稱的「單一神教」（henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（kathenotheism）。在吠陀的頌歌中，當一位神祇被召喚時，祂往往被讚頌為至高無上的、獨一無二的存在，其他神祇的功能與地位暫時被忽略或從屬於祂。這並非嚴格意義上的一神教（monotheism），因為它並不否認其他神祇的存在；它也不是多神教（polytheism）那種固定的神靈等級制。它是一種崇拜上的「單一主神論」，隨著祈禱者的需求與情境而轉換其崇拜的焦點。這種現象，正是宗教語言在其早期隱喻階段的一種典型表現，也為後來更為系統化的神學思考鋪平了道路。當這種詩性的、流動的崇拜方式逐漸凝固，並與特定的儀式、部落傳統結合時，便產生了我們所熟知的、擁有萬神殿與複雜神話體系的印度教多神教。因此，印度的宗教成長，從吠陀的自然崇拜，歷經單一神教的過渡，最終走向奧義書的哲學一元論與大眾的偶像崇拜，為「宗教學」（the science of religion）提供了一個無可比擬的比較案例，展現了人類心靈在探尋神聖時所經歷的種種形態。
沒有任何國家能與印度相比，能為真正研究宗教的起源與成長提供如此良機。我特意說「成長」，而非「宗教史」：因為就該詞的一般意義而言，歷史在印度文學中幾乎是未知的。然而，我們在印度比在任何其他地方更能觀察與研究的，是宗教思想與宗教語言如何產生、如何獲得力量、如何傳播，在口耳相傳、心靈相傳之際改變其形式，卻始終與最初湧現的源頭保持某種隱約的連續性。
因此，我認為自己並未誇大其詞：當我說印度的聖典為宗教的整體研究，特別是為宗教的起源與發展研究，提供了與印度語言——梵文——為人類語言的起源與發展研究所提供的同樣獨特且意想不到的優勢時。正是基於這個理由，我選擇了印度的古老宗教，來為我自己關於宗教起源與發展的理論提供歷史例證。
宗教。這個理論是在我終生研究印度聖典的過程中浮現的；因此，它奠基於事實，儘管我對這些事實的解釋負有責任。

我絕非主張宗教的起源與發展在各地都必須與印度完全相同。讓我們在此也從語言科學中汲取教訓。如今已無人否認，對於語言研究者必須解決的一些最晦澀的問題而言，沒有什麼比批判性地研究梵文更有用了。我甚至更進一步主張，為了充分理解其他語言所採用的方式與手段，沒有什麼比能夠將它們與梵文的運作方式進行對比更有益處。但是，像博普那樣在馬來語、玻里尼西亞語和高加索方言中尋找梵文，或者想像雅利安語言所採用的語法手段是實現人類言語目標的唯一可能手段，都將是致命的錯誤；我們必須從一開始就防範在對人類宗教進行科學研究時出現類似的危險。當我們瞭解到古印度居民如何獲得他們的宗教觀念、如何精心闡述、改變、敗壞這些觀念時，我們或許可以說，其他民族也可能從同樣的起點出發，並經歷了同樣的變遷。但我們絕不應重蹈那些人的覆轍——他們因為在非洲、美洲和澳洲文化最低落的種族中發現（或自以為發現）了物神崇拜，就斷定每一個未開化的種族在其宗教歷程中都必定始於物神崇拜。
那麼，我們能在哪些文獻中研究印度早期雅利安定居者宗教的起源與發展呢？¹ 梵語文學的發現。

印度古代文獻的發現，對多數人而言聽來更像童話而非歷史篇章，我也不訝異人們對此文獻的真實性——無論現在或至少長期以來——始終抱持某種懷疑。現存手稿的梵文獨立著作數量，目前估計約達一萬部。倘若有人告訴柏拉圖與亞里斯多德，在他們所處的時代，印度——那個亞歷山大剛發現（若非征服）的印度——竟存在著遠比他們當時在希臘所擁有的任何文獻更為豐富的古代典籍，他們又會作何感想？¹¹ ¹¹ 拉金德拉拉爾‧米特拉，《孟加拉亞洲學會圖書館梵文手稿目錄》，1877年，序言，第I頁。據稱印度事務部圖書館藏有4093部獨立抄本；博德利圖書館藏有854部；柏林圖書館藏有大約相同數量。坦賈武爾大君圖書館估計藏有超過18000部，使用十一種不同字母書寫；貝拿勒斯梵文學院圖書館藏有2000部；加爾各答孟加拉亞洲學會圖書館藏有3700部；加爾各答梵文學院圖書館藏有134部。
